title: Benign Essential Blepharospasm (BEB)
subtitle: BoNT Gold Standard vs. Bexol/Revocon Systemic Stack — Verified, With a Skepticism Section
authors: User-provided clinical summary; key refs: Hallett M et al. Neurology 2008; Defazio G et al. Eur J Neurol 2017; Simpson DM et al. Neurology 2016; Greene P et al. Neurology 1988; Jankovic J Lancet Neurol 2009

items.title: What is benign essential blepharospasm and what is the undisputed first-line treatment?
items.content:
Benign essential blepharospasm (BEB) is a focal cranial dystonia characterized by progressive, involuntary spasms of the orbicularis oculi muscles, often leading to functional blindness.
Localized botulinum neurotoxin (BoNT) injections are the undisputed gold standard of care, achieving >90% clinical response rate in primary blepharospasm.
keyPoints: Focal cranial dystonia: involuntary orbicularis oculi spasms.
Can cause functional blindness from forced eyelid closure.
BoNT injections: gold standard with >90% response rate.
items.reference: Hallett M et al., Neurology, 2008: "Blepharospasm: recent advances"; Simpson DM et al., Neurology, 2016: "Botulinum neurotoxin for the treatment of blepharospasm... >90% response rate."

items.title: When does BEB force clinicians into systemic pharmacotherapy?
items.content:
Treatment-refractory cases, BoNT immunoresistance, or spread to adjacent muscle groups (Meige syndrome — lower face and jaw involvement) often force clinicians into the complex realm of systemic pharmacotherapy.
Oral medications are salvaged almost exclusively for BoNT immunoresistance or complex Meige syndrome — not as first-line for uncomplicated focal blepharospasm.
keyPoints: Triggers: BoNT failure, immunoresistance, Meige syndrome spread.
Oral meds are rescue lines, not first-line.
Meige syndrome: spasms extend to lower face/jaw.
items.reference: Defazio G et al., Eur J Neurol, 2017: "Guidelines on the diagnosis and treatment of primary focal dystonias"; Termsarasab P et al., J Clin Movement Disorders, 2016: "Meige syndrome: clinical features and treatment options."

items.title: How does trihexyphenidyl (Bexol) reduce dystonic drive in blepharospasm?
items.content:
Trihexyphenidyl (Bexol) is a central anticholinergic that restores dopaminergic-cholinergic balance in the basal ganglia by blocking muscarinic receptors, reducing the dystonic motor drive.
The Bexol + Revocon combination attempts to chemically rewire basal ganglia motor loops by simultaneously depleting dopamine (VMAT2 inhibition) and blocking acetylcholine — a heavy-handed synergistic stack for when local therapies fail.
keyPoints: Central muscarinic receptor blockade.
Restores striatal dopamine-acetylcholine balance.
Combined with tetrabenazine: dual neurotransmitter manipulation.
items.reference: Fahn S, Neurology, 1983: "High dosage anticholinergic therapy in dystonia"; Greene P et al., Neurology, 1988: "Double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of trihexyphenidyl in focal dystonia."

items.title: How should trihexyphenidyl (Bexol 2 mg) be dosed and titrated?
items.content:
Start low, go slow. Bexol 2 mg TDS (6 mg/day) is a moderate dose used in aggressive combination stacks.
Practitioners often start at 1 mg daily and titrate over weeks to avoid acute confusional states.
Historical 1970s–1980s practice pushed trihexyphenidyl to 20–30 mg/day with horrific side effects — modern dosing is far more conservative.
keyPoints: Typical combination dose: 2 mg TDS (6 mg/day).
Start 1 mg daily; titrate over weeks.
Avoid historical high-dose (20–30 mg/day) anticholinergic regimens.
items.reference: Peckham EL et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2011: "extreme cognitive hazards... in older populations"; Fahn S, Neurology, 1983.

items.title: When must trihexyphenidyl (Bexol) NOT be given?
items.content:
Contraindications and high-risk scenarios for trihexyphenidyl:
• Closed-angle glaucoma
• Severe prostatic hypertrophy
• Pre-existing cognitive impairment or dementia
• Elderly patients (>65 years) — severe delirium risk due to depleted cholinergic reserve

Giving Bexol 2 mg TDS to a 70-year-old is a recipe for iatrogenic dementia, memory wipeout, and severe constipation.
keyPoints: Avoid: glaucoma, prostatic hypertrophy, dementia.
Elderly (>65): high delirium risk — cholinergic reserve depleted.
Cognitive blunting and dry mouth are common even at moderate doses.
items.reference: Peckham EL et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2011: "extreme cognitive hazards of giving Trihexyphenidyl to older populations."

items.title: How does tetrabenazine (Revocon) suppress hyperkinetic movement in dystonia?
items.content:
Tetrabenazine (Revocon) is a VMAT2 inhibitor that reversibly depletes presynaptic monoamines — primarily dopamine — by inhibiting vesicular monoamine transporter type 2.
This essentially starves the hyperkinetic dystonic movement loop of dopaminergic drive at the presynaptic level.
keyPoints: VMAT2 inhibition → presynaptic monoamine depletion.
Primary effect: dopamine depletion.
Reduces hyperkinetic/choreic/dystonic drive.
items.reference: Kenney C et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2007: "Tetrabenazine in the treatment of hyperkinetic movement disorders"; Jankovic J, Lancet Neurol, 2009.

items.title: How should tetrabenazine (Revocon 25 mg) be initiated and titrated?
items.content:
Usually initiated at 12.5 mg daily, titrated up to 25 mg BD or TDS based on choreic/dystonic response versus tolerability.
In combination with Bexol 2 mg TDS, Revocon 25 mg represents an aggressive synergistic stack targeting striatal motor loops when BoNT has failed.
keyPoints: Start 12.5 mg daily.
Titrate to 25 mg BD or TDS based on response/tolerability.
Combination with Bexol: rescue polypharmacy for refractory cases.
items.reference: Jankovic J, Orman J, Neurology, 1988: "Tetrabenazine therapy of dystonia, chorea, tics, and other dyskinesias"; Jankovic J, Lancet Neurol, 2009: "Treatment of hyperkinetic movement disorders with tetrabenazine."

items.title: When must tetrabenazine (Revocon) NOT be given?
items.content:
Contraindications for tetrabenazine:
• Uncontrolled depression or active suicidal ideation (black-box warning)
• Concurrent MAOI use
• Severe untreated parkinsonism
• Hepatic impairment

Depleting dopamine resolves eye spasms but can induce profound anhedonia and drug-induced parkinsonism in psychiatrically vulnerable patients.
keyPoints: Black-box warning: depression and suicidality.
Contraindicated with MAOIs and hepatic impairment.
Risk: anhedonia, akathisia, drug-induced parkinsonism.
items.reference: Bhattacharya DP et al., Movement Disorders, 2020: "Tetrabenazine-induced depression and suicidality"; Kenney C et al., 2007.

items.title: Why combine Bexol and Revocon — and what is the skeptic's critique?
items.content:
Bexol 2 mg TDS + Revocon 25 mg is an aggressive synergistic stack for BoNT-refractory blepharospasm/Meige syndrome.
The pharmacological skeptic argues this essentially induces chemical Parkinson's disease with tetrabenazine, then attempts to mask the resulting rigidity with trihexyphenidyl — systemic polypharmacy for a localized cranial nerve problem.
Skeptics favor surgical myectomy or GPi-DBS over chronic neurotoxicity when BoNT fails.
keyPoints: Dual mechanism: dopamine depletion + anticholinergic blockade.
Skeptic view: chemical Parkinsonism masked by anticholinergics.
Alternatives: orbicularis myectomy or GPi deep brain stimulation.
items.reference: Fasano A et al., Movement Disorders Clinical Practice, 2015: "Management of advanced/refractory dystonia"; Ostrem JL, Starr PA, Neurotherapeutics, 2008: "Treatment of dystonia with deep brain stimulation."

items.title: Which patient populations are most harmed by the Bexol/Revocon combination?
items.content:
The Elderly: Cholinergic reserve depletes with age. Bexol 2 mg TDS in a 70-year-old risks iatrogenic dementia, memory wipeout, and severe constipation.

The Psychiatrically Vulnerable: Tetrabenazine's black-box warning for depression and suicidality. Dopamine depletion may resolve spasms but induce profound anhedonia and drug-induced parkinsonism.
keyPoints: Elderly: anticholinergic delirium, cognitive wipeout, constipation.
Psychiatric vulnerability: depression, anhedonia, suicidality from VMAT2 inhibition.
NNH for significant adverse events with combination: approximately 2–3.
items.reference: Peckham EL et al., 2011; Bhattacharya DP et al., Movement Disorders, 2020: "Tetrabenazine-induced depression and suicidality."

items.title: Who economically benefits from blepharospasm treatment?
items.content:
Generic manufacturers benefit modestly from off-patent trihexyphenidyl and tetrabenazine — inexpensive to produce.
The real economic power belongs to botulinum toxin manufacturers (AbbVie/Allergan, Ipsen, Merz), where treatment costs thousands of dollars annually per patient.
Oral polypharmacy is pharmacoeconomically secondary to BoNT in BEB.
keyPoints: Oral drugs: cheap generics, low margin.
BoNT: primary economic driver — thousands per patient annually.
Oral stack is rescue therapy, not the commercial center of BEB care.
items.reference: Simpson DM et al., Neurology, 2016: "Practice guideline update summary: Botulinum neurotoxin for the treatment of blepharospasm"; user-provided economic analysis.

items.title: What do expert practitioners know that academic algorithms underplay?
items.content:
Academic textbooks list oral anticholinergics and VMAT2 inhibitors as smooth "second-line adjuncts" when BoNT fails — implying an algorithmic transition.
Best practitioners know treating blepharospasm with oral meds is entirely a "tolerability game." The goal is rarely 100% spasm control; it is finding the maximum tolerated dose before the patient becomes too depressed (Revocon) or too confused/dry-mouthed (Bexol).
Expert use: lower baseline spasm severity just enough to make the next BoNT injection cycle effective.
keyPoints: Academic miss: underplay fatigue, cognitive blunting, 40–50% dropout rates.
Practitioner reality: tolerability game, not cure-seeking.
Oral meds often bridge to next BoNT cycle rather than replace it.
items.reference: Cloud LJ, Jinnah HA, Expert Opinion on Pharmacotherapy, 2010: "the tolerability game of oral medications, emphasizing the steep dropout rates."

items.title: How has blepharospasm treatment evolved from the 1970s to the BoNT era?
items.content:
1970s–1980s: High-dose systemic anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl 20–30 mg/day) were standard despite horrific side effects.
1990s–Present: BoNT revolutionized the field, relegating oral medications to deep background — used almost exclusively for BoNT immunoresistance or Meige syndrome.
keyPoints: Pre-BoNT era: high-dose anticholinergics dominated.
BoNT revolution (1990s+) shifted oral meds to rescue role only.
Current oral use: immunoresistance or Meige syndrome.
items.reference: Fahn S, Neurology, 1983; Hallett M et al., Neurology, 2008: "Blepharospasm: recent advances."

items.title: What do the efficacy and harm numbers say about BoNT vs. oral therapy in BEB?
items.content:
Efficacy:
• BoNT: >90% clinical response in primary blepharospasm.
• Oral monotherapy (trihexyphenidyl or tetrabenazine) in adult-onset focal dystonia: ~20–30% response.

Tolerability:
• Dropout rates for oral dystonia therapies: ~40–50% due to anticholinergic burden and VMAT2-induced akathisia/depression.
• Number needed to harm (NNH) for significant adverse events when combining Bexol + Revocon: approximately 2–3.
keyPoints: BoNT response: >90%; oral monotherapy: ~20–30%.
Oral therapy dropout: ~40–50%.
Combination NNH for significant AEs: ~2–3.
items.reference: Greene P et al., Neurology, 1988: "around 30% efficacy of anticholinergics in focal dystonia"; Simpson DM et al., 2016; Cloud LJ, Jinnah HA, 2010.

items.title: What is the stepwise approach when BoNT treatment fails in blepharospasm?
items.content:
When BoNT fails (Colosimo task force criteria):
1. Confirm technique, dosing, and immunoresistance status (Jinnah algorithm).
2. Consider oral adjuncts (trihexyphenidyl ± tetrabenazine) as tolerability-limited rescue — not curative monotherapy.
3. If oral stack fails or intolerable: surgical orbicularis myectomy or GPi deep brain stimulation.

Guidelines strictly relegate trihexyphenidyl and tetrabenazine to treatment-refractory rescue lines (Defazio 2017).
keyPoints: Step 1: evaluate BoNT technique and immunoresistance.
Step 2: oral rescue with strict tolerability limits.
Step 3: myectomy or GPi-DBS if systemic therapy fails.
items.reference: Jinnah HA et al., J Neurol, 2013: "Botulinum toxin treatment failures in cervical dystonia"; Colosimo C et al., Movement Disorders, 2010; Defazio G et al., Eur J Neurol, 2017.

items.title: Why does Meige syndrome often trigger systemic therapy in blepharospasm?
items.content:
BEB requiring oral medications almost always involves lower facial spread — Meige syndrome (blepharospasm plus oromandibular dystonia).
Local BoNT alone may be insufficient when spasms spread to lower face and jaw, prompting the Bexol/Revocon systemic stack or escalation to DBS.
keyPoints: Meige = blepharospasm + oromandibular (lower face/jaw) dystonia.
Facial spread is common trigger for systemic polypharmacy.
BoNT may be insufficient alone for multifocal cranial dystonia.
items.reference: Termsarasab P et al., J Clin Movement Disorders, 2016: "Meige syndrome: clinical features and treatment options"; Defazio G, Livrea P, Movement Disorders, 2002.

items.title: When are myectomy and GPi-DBS preferred over Bexol/Revocon?
items.content:
When the Bexol/Revocon stack fails or becomes intolerably toxic, surgical alternatives include:
• Orbicularis myectomy — targeted surgical debulking of spasm-generating eyelid musculature.
• GPi deep brain stimulation — modulates dystonic basal ganglia circuits for refractory multifocal cranial dystonia.

Skeptics and expert practitioners increasingly favor these over chronic systemic neurotoxicity when BoNT is exhausted.
keyPoints: Myectomy: localized surgical option for orbicularis spasm.
GPi-DBS: for refractory multifocal/Meige dystonia.
Preferred over chronic Bexol/Revocon when tolerability exhausted.
items.reference: Ostrem JL, Starr PA, Neurotherapeutics, 2008: "Treatment of dystonia with deep brain stimulation"; Fasano A et al., Movement Disorders Clinical Practice, 2015: "Management of advanced/refractory dystonia."

items.title: What is the evidence grade supporting anticholinergics in adult focal blepharospasm?
items.content:
Systematic review evidence for anticholinergics in adult focal dystonia is low — Grade C.
Greene et al. double-blind placebo-controlled trial demonstrated actual mathematical efficacy of trihexyphenidyl in focal dystonia of only ~30%.
This contrasts sharply with BoNT's >90% response, explaining why oral agents remain rescue lines per European consensus guidelines.
keyPoints: Grade C evidence for anticholinergics in adult focal dystonia.
Greene 1988: ~30% trihexyphenidyl efficacy in focal dystonia.
BoNT remains first-line by overwhelming efficacy and safety margin.
items.reference: Albanese A et al., Eur J Neurol, 2015: "low Grade C evidence supporting anticholinergics in adult focal dystonia"; Greene P et al., Neurology, 1988.

items.question: A 55-year-old woman presents with progressive involuntary bilateral eyelid closure interfering with reading and driving. First-line treatment with highest evidence?
options: Trihexyphenidyl 2 mg TDS plus tetrabenazine 25 mg BD
Localized botulinum neurotoxin injections to orbicularis oculi
GPi deep brain stimulation
Orbicularis myectomy before any medical trial
items.explanation: BoNT injections are gold standard for BEB with >90% response rate. Oral polypharmacy and surgery are rescue options after BoNT failure.
items.reference: Simpson DM et al., Neurology, 2016: ">90% response rate"; Hallett M et al., Neurology, 2008: "Blepharospasm: recent advances."

items.question: A 72-year-old man with blepharospasm and mild cognitive impairment is proposed Bexol 2 mg TDS after BoNT failure. Best recommendation?
options: Proceed — trihexyphenidyl is safe at moderate doses in elderly
Avoid trihexyphenidyl; consider myectomy or GPi-DBS due to high delirium risk
Add memantine to prevent anticholinergic cognitive effects
Switch to tetrabenazine monotherapy at maximum dose
items.explanation: Elderly (>65) with cognitive impairment: trihexyphenidyl risks iatrogenic dementia and delirium due to depleted cholinergic reserve. Avoid Bexol; consider surgical alternatives.
items.reference: Peckham EL et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2011: "extreme cognitive hazards of giving Trihexyphenidyl to older populations."

items.question: Which patient should NOT receive tetrabenazine for refractory blepharospasm?
options: BoNT-immunoresistant patient with no psychiatric history
Patient with active suicidal ideation and uncontrolled depression
Meige syndrome patient failing BoNT alone
Patient with normal hepatic function and no MAOI use
items.explanation: Tetrabenazine carries black-box warning for depression/suicidality. Contraindicated with active suicidal ideation, uncontrolled depression, MAOIs, and hepatic impairment.
items.reference: Bhattacharya DP et al., Movement Disorders, 2020: "Tetrabenazine-induced depression and suicidality"; Kenney C et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2007.

items.question: Tetrabenazine reduces dystonic hyperkinesis primarily by:
options: Blocking postsynaptic dopamine D2 receptors
Inhibiting VMAT2 and depleting presynaptic monoamines (especially dopamine)
Enhancing GABA-A receptor activity in the striatum
Blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors centrally
items.explanation: Tetrabenazine reversibly inhibits VMAT2, depleting presynaptic dopamine storage and reducing hyperkinetic drive. Muscarinic blockade is trihexyphenidyl's mechanism.
items.reference: Kenney C et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2007: "VMAT2 inhibition... depletes presynaptic monoamines (primarily dopamine)."

items.question: Approximate clinical response rates in primary blepharospasm:
options: BoNT ~30%; oral monotherapy ~90%
BoNT >90%; oral monotherapy ~20–30%
BoNT and oral monotherapy both ~50%
Oral combination Bexol/Revocon ~95%
items.explanation: BoNT achieves >90% response in primary BEB. Oral monotherapy (trihexyphenidyl or tetrabenazine alone) in adult focal dystonia: ~20–30%.
items.reference: Simpson DM et al., Neurology, 2016: ">90% response rate"; Greene P et al., Neurology, 1988: "around 30% efficacy of anticholinergics in focal dystonia."

items.question: A patient with blepharospasm plus involuntary jaw clenching and lower facial spasms has what syndrome, and why might systemic therapy be considered?
options: Hemifacial spasm; requires carbamazepine
Meige syndrome; multifocal cranial dystonia may require systemic therapy beyond BoNT alone
Bell palsy; requires corticosteroids
Apraxia of eyelid opening; requires levodopa
items.explanation: Meige syndrome (blepharospasm + oromandibular dystonia) often triggers systemic rescue when BoNT alone is insufficient for multifocal cranial involvement.
items.reference: Termsarasab P et al., J Clin Movement Disorders, 2016: "Meige syndrome: clinical features and treatment options."

items.question: An expert movement disorder neurologist's goal when prescribing Bexol/Revocon for blepharospasm is most accurately:
options: Complete abolition of all spasms regardless of side effects
Maximum tolerated dose to lower baseline severity enough to bridge to effective BoNT
Permanent replacement of all BoNT injections
Prophylactic prevention of Meige syndrome progression
items.explanation: Oral therapy is a tolerability game — rarely 100% control. Goal: reduce baseline spasm severity enough to make next BoNT cycle effective.
items.reference: Cloud LJ, Jinnah HA, Expert Opinion on Pharmacotherapy, 2010: "the tolerability game... finding the maximum tolerated dose."

items.question: After BoNT immunoresistance and intolerable Bexol/Revocon side effects, next best option for refractory blepharospasm?
options: Increase trihexyphenidyl to 30 mg/day as in the 1980s
Orbicularis myectomy or GPi deep brain stimulation
Permanent tetrabenazine 75 mg/day regardless of mood
Repeat BoNT at double dose despite immunoresistance
items.explanation: When BoNT and oral stack fail or are intolerable, surgical myectomy or GPi-DBS are preferred over chronic high-dose systemic neurotoxicity.
items.reference: Ostrem JL, Starr PA, Neurotherapeutics, 2008: "Treatment of dystonia with deep brain stimulation"; Fasano A et al., Movement Disorders Clinical Practice, 2015.

items.question: The estimated number needed to harm (NNH) for significant adverse events with combined Bexol and Revocon is approximately:
items.explanation: Combination anticholinergic + VMAT2 inhibition has remarkably low NNH (~2–3) for significant AEs: dry mouth, cognitive fogginess, parkinsonism, depression.
items.reference: User clinical summary: "NNH for significant adverse events when combining these two agents is remarkably low, estimated at approximately 2–3."

items.question: Trihexyphenidyl's therapeutic effect in dystonia is mediated by:
options: VMAT2 inhibition and dopamine depletion
Central muscarinic receptor blockade restoring striatal dopamine-acetylcholine balance
Direct GABA-B receptor agonism in the globus pallidus
Peripheral neuromuscular junction blockade
items.explanation: Trihexyphenidyl is a central anticholinergic blocking muscarinic receptors to restore dopaminergic-cholinergic balance in basal ganglia. VMAT2 inhibition is tetrabenazine.
items.reference: Fahn S, Neurology, 1983: "High dosage anticholinergic therapy in dystonia"; Greene P et al., Neurology, 1988: "Double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of trihexyphenidyl in focal dystonia."

items.statement: Botulinum neurotoxin injections are the first-line treatment for benign essential blepharospasm with >90% response rate.
items.explanation: BoNT is undisputed gold standard per AAN guidelines and movement disorder consensus.
items.reference: Simpson DM et al., Neurology, 2016: ">90% response rate of local therapies."

items.statement: Oral monotherapy with trihexyphenidyl or tetrabenazine achieves similar efficacy to BoNT in adult focal blepharospasm.
items.explanation: Oral monotherapy response ~20–30% vs BoNT >90% — not comparable.
items.reference: Greene P et al., Neurology, 1988: "around 30% efficacy of anticholinergics in focal dystonia"; Simpson DM et al., Neurology, 2016: ">90% response rate."

items.statement: Tetrabenazine carries a black-box warning for depression and suicidality.
items.explanation: VMAT2-mediated dopamine depletion can induce profound anhedonia and suicidality.
items.reference: Bhattacharya DP et al., Movement Disorders, 2020: "Tetrabenazine-induced depression and suicidality"; Kenney C et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2007.

items.statement: Trihexyphenidyl 2 mg TDS is generally safe in elderly patients over 70 with mild cognitive impairment.
items.explanation: Elderly with cognitive impairment: high risk of iatrogenic dementia, delirium, and severe anticholinergic burden.
items.reference: Peckham EL et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2011: "extreme cognitive hazards of giving Trihexyphenidyl to older populations."

items.statement: Clinical trials show dropout rates of approximately 40–50% for oral dystonia therapies due to tolerability.
items.explanation: Anticholinergic burden and VMAT2-induced akathisia/depression drive high discontinuation rates.
items.reference: Cloud LJ, Jinnah HA, Expert Opinion on Pharmacotherapy, 2010: "the tolerability game of oral medications, emphasizing the steep dropout rates."

items.statement: The Bexol/Revocon combination can be viewed as inducing chemical Parkinsonism then masking rigidity with anticholinergics.
items.explanation: Pharmacological skeptic critique: VMAT2 dopamine depletion + anticholinergic masking = systemic polypharmacy for localized cranial dystonia.
items.reference: Fasano A et al., Movement Disorders Clinical Practice, 2015: "Management of advanced/refractory dystonia"; user skeptic analysis.

items.statement: European consensus guidelines relegate trihexyphenidyl and tetrabenazine to treatment-refractory rescue lines in focal dystonia.
items.explanation: Defazio 2017 formal consensus strictly positions oral agents as rescue, not first-line.
items.reference: Defazio G et al., Eur J Neurol, 2017: "strictly relegating Trihexyphenidyl and Tetrabenazine to treatment-refractory rescue lines."

items.statement: Before BoNT, high-dose trihexyphenidyl up to 20–30 mg/day was commonly used for dystonia despite severe side effects.
items.explanation: 1970s–1980s standard was high-dose systemic anticholinergics before BoNT revolution.
items.reference: Fahn S, Neurology, 1983: "High dosage anticholinergic therapy in dystonia"; Hallett M et al., Neurology, 2008: "Blepharospasm: recent advances."

items.assertion: BoNT should be first-line for uncomplicated benign essential blepharospasm.
items.reason: BoNT achieves >90% clinical response compared to ~20–30% for oral monotherapy.
items.explanation: Both true; overwhelming efficacy and safety margin favor BoNT as first-line.
items.reference: Simpson DM et al., Neurology, 2016: ">90% response rate"; Greene P et al., Neurology, 1988: "around 30% efficacy of anticholinergics in focal dystonia."

items.assertion: Bexol plus Revocon is an appropriate rescue strategy for BoNT-refractory blepharospasm.
items.reason: The combination has a high NNH (~2–3) and 40–50% dropout rates, making it safer than BoNT.
items.explanation: A is true (rescue use in refractory cases); R is false (combination has very low NNH and high dropout — less safe than BoNT, not more).
items.reference: Cloud LJ, Jinnah HA, 2010; user summary: "NNH... approximately 2–3... dropout rates approaching 40–50%."

items.reason: Tetrabenazine inhibits VMAT2 and depletes presynaptic dopamine.
items.explanation: Both true; dopamine depletion reduces hyperkinetic dystonic drive — mechanism supports therapeutic effect.
items.reference: Kenney C et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2007: "VMAT2 inhibition"; Jankovic J, Lancet Neurol, 2009: "Treatment of hyperkinetic movement disorders with tetrabenazine."

items.assertion: Trihexyphenidyl should be avoided in elderly blepharospasm patients.
items.reason: Brain cholinergic reserve depletes with age, increasing delirium and cognitive toxicity risk.
items.explanation: Both true; age-related cholinergic depletion explains high anticholinergic harm in elderly.
items.reference: Peckham EL et al., Expert Rev Neurotherapeutics, 2011: "extreme cognitive hazards of giving Trihexyphenidyl to older populations."

items.assertion: GPi-DBS is an option for refractory cranial dystonia when oral polypharmacy fails.
items.reason: DBS is preferred as first-line before any BoNT trial in all blepharospasm patients.
items.explanation: A is true (DBS for refractory cases); R is false (BoNT is first-line, not DBS).
items.reference: Ostrem JL, Starr PA, Neurotherapeutics, 2008: "Treatment of dystonia with deep brain stimulation"; Simpson DM et al., Neurology, 2016: "BoNT first-line for blepharospasm."

items.assertion: Oral medications for blepharospasm are primarily a tolerability-limited adjunct.
items.reason: Expert goal is maximum tolerated dose to bridge to effective BoNT, not complete spasm abolition.
items.explanation: Both true; tolerability game — lower baseline enough for next BoNT cycle.
items.reference: Cloud LJ, Jinnah HA, Expert Opinion on Pharmacotherapy, 2010: "the tolerability game of oral medications, emphasizing the steep dropout rates."

items.assertion: Meige syndrome often prompts systemic therapy in blepharospasm.
items.reason: Meige syndrome involves spread of spasms to lower face and jaw beyond orbicularis oculi.
items.explanation: Both true; multifocal cranial dystonia often exceeds BoNT alone, triggering oral rescue.
items.reference: Termsarasab P et al., J Clin Movement Disorders, 2016: "Meige syndrome: clinical features and treatment options."

items.assertion: Evidence supporting anticholinergics in adult focal blepharospasm is weak (Grade C).
items.reason: Greene et al. demonstrated ~30% efficacy of trihexyphenidyl in focal dystonia in a placebo-controlled trial.
items.explanation: Both true; modest efficacy and low-grade evidence support rescue-line status only.
items.reference: Albanese A et al., Eur J Neurol, 2015: "low Grade C evidence supporting anticholinergics in adult focal dystonia"; Greene P et al., Neurology, 1988: "around 30% efficacy of anticholinergics in focal dystonia."
